feedback.text: I am writing to express serious concern about the increasing lack of consumer rights and transparency in the digital goods market, particularly regarding digital games, software, movies, and music. The current system undermines basic ownership principles and disproportionately favors platform holders and publishers over consumers. In the digital age, consumers are often misled into believing they are purchasing a product when in reality they are only licensing access an access that can be revoked, altered, or removed at any time, often without warning or recourse. This practice is fundamentally unfair, especially when users are paying full price for what appears to be a permanent product. One clear example of this is the growing trend of video games being delisted or made entirely unplayable after servers are shut down or licenses expire. Games like The Crew, Battleborn, or Scott Pilgrim vs. The World have been completely removed or made inaccessible, even to those who purchased them legally. This isnt just an inconvenience it erodes consumer trust and raises significant concerns about preservation, transparency, and the actual value of digital purchases. These issues are not limited to games. Movies, TV shows, and music bought digitally are also subject to license removals, with companies like Amazon and Apple quietly deleting paid-for content from users libraries due to rights changes. Consumers are not properly informed of these risks at the time of purchase, and the contractual terms are often hidden in lengthy, confusing Terms of Service documents. I urge the Commission to consider the following measures as part of the Digital Fairness Act: 1. Clear labeling and language: Require platforms to explicitly state when a product is a license, not a purchase, and what rights the consumer has (including whether access can be revoked). 2. Right to permanence: Digital products that are paid for at full retail price should come with guaranteed offline access where applicable, or long-term availability provisions, regardless of server or licensing status. 3. Transparency in delisting: Mandate consumer notifications and refunds (or alternatives) when a digital product is delisted or removed from access. 4. Support for preservation: Protect the right of consumers or third parties (e.g., museums, preservationists, nonprofits) to maintain or emulate digital products that have been officially discontinued, especially where no legal access exists. 5. End to deceptive practices: Ensure platforms are not using manipulative UI or dark patterns to hide these risks at the point of sale. If we do not act now, we risk setting a precedent where consumers no longer own anything they merely rent access in a system designed to benefit corporations at the expense of culture, history, and user rights. The EU has an opportunity to set global standards here, and I strongly encourage the Commission to take a bold stand in favor of digital fairness, transparency, and true ownership. The most important of them is, in my humble opinion, true ownership. In a world where you merely gain access to a license through a service, despite having paid full retail price for it, and being subject to the loss of that access at any time or for no reason, is a world where consumer have been subjugated to a monolopy! And, there is a ban on ABUSE OF A DOMINANT POSITION ex. Art. 102 TFEU (EN) VWEU (NL) in the EU, i would like to see that encorced! What makes me say that there is an abuse of this dominant position on the licensing of such content, with special regards to, DENTING ACCES TO THE CONTENT? The dominant position, more specifically, the abuse of it, lies in that most media (Movie, music, games and TV Shows) are bought digitally! And there have been plenty of cases where consumers have lost the access to the media they bought. With that said, i truly hope to see consumers protected once again! Thank you for your attention to this matter.
